title: Feature Request: Optional serpbase.dev integration as a SERP data source backend
author: gefsikatsinelou
body:
Hi @towfiqi,

SerpBear has been a fantastic self-hosted alternative to expensive rank-tracking SaaS tools. The UI is clean and the cron-based tracking workflow fits perfectly into my stack.

**Current friction**
The biggest operational headache I have is keeping the scraping layer healthy. Google changes markup frequently, and running from a single IP (even with modest volumes) eventually triggers blocks or CAPTCHAs. I've had to add proxy rotation and headless Chrome, which increases resource usage and complexity — somewhat defeating the "simple self-hosted" appeal.

**Idea**
What do you think about adding an optional https://serpbase.dev backend? Users who prefer zero-maintenance could flip a switch and pull structured SERP data from the API instead of scraping directly. Those who want to keep scraping for cost reasons could still do so.

**Why it makes sense for SerpBear**
- SerpBear is already a "SERP tool"; the API is a natural complement rather than a departure.
- It would make the project viable for users on shared hosting or low-resource VPS who can't run a heavy scraping pipeline.
- The API returns exactly the fields SerpBear needs (position, title, URL, snippet), so the data mapping is 1:1.

Maybe a new env var like `SERP_SOURCE=serpbase` with an `SERPBASE_KEY` could be the toggle.

Thanks for considering!

title: keyword volume | research groups
author: ARCICT
body:
Hello,

There is also a formatting issue, for example: 201,000 (or 2,001,000) seems to be shown as 2,01,000

We might completely miss it, but how do you create groups in “research keywords”? We've added groups in the past, but can't seem to find the way to create a group and/or add keywords any more.

Thank you

title: Add support for additional low-cost SERP API provider (SerpBase)
author: itzuimao
body:
Hi,

First of all, thanks for building SerpBear — it’s one of the cleanest self-hosted SERP tracking tools I’ve used.

I’ve been using it in a few small projects, and I ran into a practical limitation that I think many users eventually face: the cost of SERP API providers scales up very quickly compared to actual usage needs, especially for indie developers or early-stage SaaS.

Current situation

Most supported providers (like SerpAPI / SearchAPI) are reliable, but:

pricing is relatively high for continuous keyword tracking
small projects often end up overpaying just to keep data flowing
self-hosting SerpBear becomes less “cost-independent” in practice
Suggested improvement

It would be great if SerpBear could support an additional lightweight / low-cost provider option, for example something like SerpBase(https://serpbase.dev).

From my testing, it’s:

significantly cheaper for high-frequency queries
simple API structure (easy to integrate as a drop-in provider)
suitable for users who care more about cost efficiency than enterprise-grade SLAs
Why this could help SerpBear

Adding another provider option would:

make SerpBear more accessible for indie hackers / solo devs
give users a cost-performance choice instead of a single dependency path
improve resilience by not locking users into one pricing model

Even a basic integration (same interface as existing providers) would already be very useful.

If this is something you'd consider, I can help test or provide integration feedback from a real usage perspective.

Thanks again for the project 🙏

title: Using .env with USER can cause login errors
author: Gre3nGr3en
body:
Hi! I tried using a `.env` file to set my credentials when running SerpBear via Docker Compose, and I ran into an "Incorrect Username" error on every login attempt.

**What happened:**
The issue comes from using `USER` as a variable name. On Linux, `USER` is a reserved system environment variable that always holds the current OS user (e.g. `root` or `ubuntu`). Because of this, any value set for `USER` in a `.env` file is ignored inside the container, so `USER_NAME` never receives the intended username.

**Workaround:**

1. Rename the variable in your `.env` file:

```bash
# Before
USER=your_username

# After
APP_USER=your_username
```

2. Update `docker-compose.yaml`:

```yaml
# Before
- USER_NAME=${USER:-admin}

# After
- USER_NAME=${APP_USER:-admin}
```

3. Restart the container:

```bash
docker compose down
docker compose up -d
```

Hope this helps someone, or gets fixed in a future update! 🙂

title: Docker image bakes NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL at build time, ignoring runtime env
author: capocasa
body:
## Problem

The pre-built Docker image hardcodes `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=http://localhost:3000` into the compiled Next.js output at build time. Setting `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` as a runtime environment variable (via docker-compose or `.env`) has no effect on server-side API routes.

This breaks the Google Ads OAuth flow when running behind a reverse proxy — the redirect URL is always `http://localhost:3000/api/adwords` regardless of the env var.

## Evidence

In the running container:

```
$ grep localhost:3000 /app/.next/server/pages/api/adwords.js
redirectURL = `${"http://localhost:3000".replace(/\/$/, "")}/api/adwords`;
```

The env var is correctly set in the container (`printenv NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` returns the right value), but Next.js inlined it at build time.

## Workaround

```bash
docker exec serpbear-app-1 sed -i "s|http://localhost:3000|https://your.domain.com|g" /app/.next/server/pages/api/adwords.js
docker compose restart
```

This patch is lost on recreate/image pull.

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. Add `ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` + `ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` before `npm run build` in the Dockerfile builder stage, so users can pass it as a build arg
2. Use `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` via a server-only env var (e.g. `APP_URL`) that isn't subject to Next.js build-time inlining

Are you able to assist?

title: v3.0.0 Google Ads OAuth redirect_uri_mismatch hardcodes localhost in compiled /api/adwords
author: robetus
body:
## Title

v3.0.0 Google Ads OAuth redirect_uri_mismatch hardcodes localhost in compiled `/api/adwords`

## Description

In SerpBear v3.0.0, Google Ads OAuth appears broken behind a reverse proxy because the compiled `/api/adwords` route hardcodes `http://localhost:3000` as the redirect URL.

This causes:

`redirect_uri_mismatch`

with:

`Redirected URL: http://localhost:3000/api/adwords`

## Environment

- SerpBear: v3.0.0
- Docker / Portainer
- Reverse proxy: Nginx Proxy Manager
- Public URL: `https://serp.example.com`

## Runtime env is correct

Inside the running container:

```bash
docker exec -it serpbear-app-1 printenv | grep NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL
```

Output:

```bash
NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=https://serp.example.com
```

## Compiled file in v3.0.0 is wrong

Command:

```bash
docker exec -it serpbear-app-1 sh -c "grep -n 'localhost:3000\\|redirectURL\\|NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL' /app/.next/server/pages/api/adwords.js | head -50"
```

Output in v3.0.0 included:

```js
redirectURL = `${"http://localhost:3000".replace(/\/$/, "")}/api/adwords`;
```

This appears to mean the compiled build is baking in localhost instead of using the runtime public URL.

## Downgrading fixes it

After downgrading one version, the compiled file changed to:

```js
const redirectURL = `${https}${req.headers.host}/api/adwords`;
```

So the localhost hardcoding appears to be a regression in v3.0.0.

## Expected behavior

The Google Ads OAuth redirect URL should use the public app URL or forwarded host/proto headers, not hardcoded localhost.

## Notes

I already verified:

- reverse proxy headers
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=https://serp.example.com`
- exact Google OAuth redirect URI

The issue reproduced on v3.0.0 and stopped after downgrading back to 2.0.7.

body:
Hello,

A) We get random errors during scraping
2026-03-05 15:07:56 [0] [ERROR] Scraping page 1 for keyword: appelsien bruxelles Unexpected token '<', "<html><bod"... is not valid JSON
2026-03-05 15:08:03 [0] [SUCCESS] Updating the Keyword:  appelsien bruxelles
...
2026-03-05 15:20:36 [0] [ERROR] Scraping page 2 for keyword: appelsien brussels Unexpected token 's', "stream timeout" is not valid JSON
2026-03-05 15:20:36 [0] [SUCCESS] Updating the Keyword:  appelsien brussels

B) The logs show [SUCCESS] but the GUI indicates “Error Updating Keyword position”

C) If we set, for example, scrape strategy to custom to 10 pages, we get lots of skipped positions, in this example “10 results scraped • 90 positions skipped”.
If we set it, for example, to two pages, sometimes we get results of the first page (second one skipped), sometimes we get results of the second page (first page skipped).

We played around with the settings and scrape strategies without success.
We use scraping robot for scrapes.

Can you tell us how to see the full log so we can examine the JSON?

title: Wildcard subdomain

I added https://site.com, and it detects the subdomain https://www.site.com but not the other two subdomains. I tried adding **https://*.site.com**, but this created a bug where the domain options are no longer accessible (it appears as %2A.site.com on the left). This means I cannot delete it either. Thanks!

title: Domain with hyphen not matching Google results - ranking shows 100+
author: SecMulSol
body:
## Bug Description

When tracking a domain that contains a hyphen (e.g. `agri-surveillance.fr`), SerpBear fails to match the domain in Google search results, even though the site clearly appears in the scraped results.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Add domain `https://agri-surveillance.fr`
2. Add keywords (e.g. "caméra stabulation")
3. Run a scrape
4. In the scraped Google results, the site `https://www.agri-surveillance.fr/` is visible
5. However, SerpBear reports a ranking of 100+ (not found)

## Expected Behavior

SerpBear should detect `www.agri-surveillance.fr` in the results and report the correct ranking position.

## Actual Behavior

The domain is not matched despite being present in the scraped results. I suspect the hyphen (`-`) in the domain name causes a regex matching issue.

## Environment

- SerpBear: latest Docker image (`towfiqi/serpbear:latest`)
- Deployment: Docker on Proxmox LXC (Debian 12)
- Scraper: ScrapingRobot

## Notes

I noticed a previous fix for hyphenated subdomains (commit c0470cf), but the issue seems to persist for hyphenated main domain names.
